references.title: ANN-LSTM: A deep learning model for early student performance prediction in MOOC

extracted.Moment of Prediction: One attempt per Day of Course Duration (270 days in total)
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The work starts with the extraction of the data from OULAD. 

Authors used One-Hot Encoding and Normalization.
extracted.Models:
LSTM-ANN,
RNN, 
GRU,
MLP, 
Random Forest, 
AML
extracted.Comments:
In total, the authors perform 270 experiments. Each experiment relates to having one dataset starting at Day 1 and Finishing at each day throughout the course duration.

Authors show that model performance increases with longer datasets.

Accuracy is computed for every day of prediction. Authors make 2 different sets of comparisons:

1- ANN-LSTM vs other DL baselines - GRU and RNN, 
2. ANN - LSTM versus other State-of-the -art architectures: MLP, RF and AML.

In both scenarios, ANN-LSTM outperforms the other modells.
extracted.Limitations:
Holdout Method, 

Computational Times for Deep Learning, which hinders a model’s ability to be used as an EWS.

annotations.text: There is a problem with current classifiers of performance as they often are done in a course or a small subset of courses at the end of the course. Moreover, binary classification seems to be the preferred task. Thses limitations hinder the ability to provide meaningful feedback and student support.

To address this gap, authors propose a day-wise model to predict performance called ANN-LSTM. Model was compared against a baseline RNN and baseline GRU model. Moreover, results were compared against other state-of-the-art methods. During model testing, it was found that, by the 3rd month of a course, ANN-LSTM obliterated the baselines, achieving 70% accuracy in comparison with the 53% and 56% of RNN and GRU.
In addition, the model also outperformed the state-of-the art models between 6% and 14%, showing LSTM as an effective way to model temporal relationships in the data.
